news_summary: Recent health research reveals breakthroughs in understanding Alzheimer's progression, new insights into ovarian function after menopause, the unique sleep patterns contributing to human success, and the role of skin compounds in mosquito attraction.
news_summary_fr: Des recherches récentes dans le domaine de la santé ont permis de réaliser des avancées décisives dans la compréhension de l'évolution de la maladie d'Alzheimer, d'apporter de nouvelles perspectives sur le fonctionnement des ovaires après la ménopause, de mettre en lumière les rythmes de sommeil spécifiques qui contribuent à la réussite humaine, et de mettre en évidence le rôle des composés cutanés dans l'attraction des moustiques.
news_summary_es: Investigaciones recientes en el ámbito de la salud revelan avances decisivos en la comprensión de la progresión del Alzheimer, nuevos conocimientos sobre la función ovárica tras la menopausia, los patrones de sueño únicos que contribuyen al éxito humano y el papel de los compuestos de la piel en la atracción de los mosquitos.

articles.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
articles.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.
articles.content:
Alzheimer's disease is marked by the buildup of a toxic protein called Tau, which damages and eventually kills brain cells. As this harmful protein moves into new areas of the brain, the disease progresses, leading to worsening memory loss and cognitive decline.

Now, researchers have uncovered an unexpected player in that process. In a study of mice, they found that a brain protein called Arc, which normally helps neurons communicate, also appears to help toxic Tau spread from diseased brain cells to healthy ones.

The discovery points to a possible new strategy for slowing Alzheimer's disease. Rather than trying to eliminate Tau entirely, future treatments might stop it from reaching healthy brain cells in the first place.

"I'm excited by the fact that we've identified a new way of potentially stopping the progression of Alzheimer's disease," says Jason Shepherd, PhD, professor of neurobiology at University of Utah Health and senior author of the study.

The findings were published in the journal Cell.

How Arc Helps Toxic Tau Travel

To investigate how Alzheimer's spreads, the researchers compared mouse models of the disease with and without the Arc protein. Their experiments showed that Arc is essential for moving toxic Tau between neurons.

Under normal conditions, Arc plays an important role in brain function. The protein packages itself inside tiny membrane bound sacs known as extracellular vesicles (EVs), which travel from one neuron to another carrying important cellular signals.

The researchers found that toxic Tau can exploit this natural communication system. By attaching itself to Arc inside these microscopic vesicles, Tau is able to travel from an unhealthy neuron into a healthy one, where it can continue spreading disease.

Tau Turns Healthy Brain Cells Toxic

Every neuron contains Tau, but in Alzheimer's disease the protein begins clumping into large, sticky tangles that interfere with the cell's internal transport system before eventually killing the neuron.

Mitali Tyagi, PhD, postdoctoral research associate at Washington University in St. Louis and first author of the study, who conducted the research while a neuroscience graduate student in the Shepherd Lab at U of U Health, compares these tangles to "glue monsters."

"They glue together and block transportation within the neuron," Tyagi explains. "But they can break down into smaller glue monsters, called Tau seeds, which can then get transferred to a new neuron. And once this Tau seed comes into contact with healthy Tau, it is able to corrupt it. So, the pathology starts all over again in a healthy neuron."

In the Alzheimer's mouse model, the team found extracellular vesicles containing both Arc and "sticky" Tau in brain tissue. These vesicles were capable of entering healthy cells and triggering the formation of new Tau tangles.

The picture changed dramatically when Arc was removed. Mice lacking the protein had extracellular vesicles containing very little Tau, and the disease could no longer spread effectively to neighboring brain cells.

"When we removed Arc, we saw that the transfer of Tau was severely, severely reduced," Tyagi says. "It was almost gone."

Arc Has Both Harmful and Helpful Effects

Although blocking Arc might sound like an obvious treatment strategy, the researchers discovered that the protein also performs an important protective role during the early stages of disease.

By helping neurons expel excess toxic Tau, Arc appears to allow damaged cells to survive longer. In mice without Arc, toxic Tau remained trapped inside neurons, causing those already sick cells to die more quickly.

"When Arc is absent, Tau becomes trapped inside neurons and accumulates to toxic levels. When Arc is present, Tau can be released in extracellular vesicles. While this helps reduce Tau buildup within the original neuron, the released Tau can be taken up by neighboring healthy neurons, promoting the spread of pathology," Tyagi says.

These findings suggest that the most effective treatment may not be preventing diseased cells from releasing Tau. Instead, it may be better to stop those toxic extracellular vesicles from entering healthy neurons.

A Potential New Target for Alzheimer's Therapies

The researchers also found extracellular vesicles containing both Arc and Tau in human brain tissue, suggesting the same mechanism could exist in people. However, they stress that much more research is needed before any potential therapy reaches patients.

"Most of the work we've been doing is in mice, not in humans," Shepherd says. "We have some clues that whatever is happening in these mice could also be happening in humans, but we don't know that yet. And we're far away from saying that we're developing a treatment for anything. But it could open new avenues to get to that point."

One promising possibility would be to intercept Tau containing extracellular vesicles after they leave diseased neurons but before they reach healthy ones. While such an approach would not reverse existing brain damage, it could potentially slow or prevent further spread of Alzheimer's disease.

"If we could target these particular EVs, that would be a really useful therapy strategy," Shepherd says. "For someone with early-onset Alzheimer's or dementia, if we could stop the spread, then we could prevent further damage and cognitive decline."

The study, titled "Arc mediates intercellular tau transmission via extracellular vesicles," was published in Cell.

The research was supported by the National Institutes of Health, including the Director's Office Transformative Research Award (R01 NS115716), the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (DSPAN F99), and the National Institute on Aging (AG073236), the Chan-Zuckerberg Initiative Ben Barres Early Acceleration Award, the Alzheimer's Association, the McKnight Brain Disorders Award, the Jon M. Huntsman Presidential Endowed Chair fund, the Max Planck Society, AIRC IG 26229, PRIN 2022EMZJL4, the Rainwater Foundation, the JPB Foundation, and the Cure Alzheimer Fund. The Massachusetts Alzheimer's Disease Research Center, supported by the National Institute on Aging (P30AG062421) provided human samples.

Shepherd is a co-founder of VNV, LLC and holds stock in and is a consultant for Aera Therapeutics, Inc., which licenses intellectual property and patents that include Arc capsids.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about how Alzheimer's disease spreads in the brain, identifying a protein (Arc) that facilitates the transmission of toxic Tau proteins. This breakthrough offers a promising new therapeutic target that could slow the progression of Alzheimer's, a disease with broad societal impact. The study provides detailed experimental evidence and potential future treatment strategies, making it a substantive and hopeful advancement in health research.
articles.personality_title: New protein discovery offers hope to slow Alzheimer’s spread
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer’s disease damages the brain by spreading harmful proteins called Tau, which kill brain cells and cause memory loss. Scientists have been trying to understand how these toxic proteins move from sick cells to healthy ones.

**What happened** – Researchers studied mice and found that a brain protein named Arc, which usually helps brain cells communicate, also carries toxic Tau proteins between neurons. Tau hides inside tiny sacs called extracellular vesicles that Arc forms, allowing Tau to travel and infect healthy brain cells. When mice lacked Arc, the spread of Tau almost stopped.

**Impact** – This discovery reveals a new way Alzheimer’s disease spreads in the brain. Rather than removing Tau completely, future treatments might block these Tau-filled sacs from reaching healthy cells. This could slow down how fast Alzheimer’s damages the brain. The research also showed that Arc helps sick cells survive longer by releasing Tau, showing the protein has both good and bad effects.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to find ways to stop Tau-containing vesicles from entering healthy neurons without harming Arc’s helpful roles. They also need to confirm if this process happens the same way in humans. If successful, new therapies might slow Alzheimer’s progression, especially for people in early stages of the disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered that the brain protein Arc helps harmful Tau proteins spread in Alzheimer’s, opening a new path to slow the disease’s progress by blocking this spread.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle protéine offre une piste pour ralentir la propagation d’Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d’Alzheimer endommage le cerveau en propageant des protéines toxiques appelées Tau, qui détruisent les cellules cérébrales et provoquent des pertes de mémoire. Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment ces protéines passent des cellules malades aux cellules saines.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié des souris et découvert qu’une protéine cérébrale nommée Arc, qui aide normalement les cellules à communiquer, transporte aussi les protéines Tau toxiques entre les neurones. Tau se cache dans de petites vésicules appelées vésicules extracellulaires que forme Arc, permettant à Tau de se déplacer et d’infecter les cellules saines. Chez les souris sans Arc, la propagation de Tau a presque cessé.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre une nouvelle façon dont Alzheimer se propage dans le cerveau. Au lieu d’éliminer complètement Tau, les futurs traitements pourraient bloquer ces vésicules contenant Tau avant qu’elles n’atteignent les cellules saines. Cela pourrait ralentir la progression des dégâts causés par Alzheimer. La recherche a aussi montré qu’Arc aide les cellules malades à survivre plus longtemps en libérant Tau, ce qui signifie que cette protéine a des effets à la fois bons et mauvais.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent trouver comment empêcher les vésicules contenant Tau d’entrer dans les neurones sains sans nuire aux fonctions bénéfiques d’Arc. Ils doivent aussi vérifier si ce mécanisme est le même chez l’humain. Si c’est le cas, de nouveaux traitements pourraient ralentir la maladie, surtout chez les personnes aux premiers stades.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que la protéine cérébrale Arc aide les protéines Tau toxiques à se propager dans Alzheimer, ouvrant une nouvelle voie pour ralentir la maladie en bloquant cette propagation.
articles.personality_title_es: Descubren proteína que podría frenar la propagación del Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer daña el cerebro al esparcir proteínas dañinas llamadas Tau, que matan células cerebrales y causan pérdida de memoria. Los científicos buscan entender cómo estas proteínas pasan de células enfermas a sanas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron ratones y hallaron que una proteína cerebral llamada Arc, que normalmente ayuda a las células a comunicarse, también transporta proteínas Tau tóxicas entre neuronas. Tau se esconde dentro de pequeñas vesículas llamadas vesículas extracelulares que Arc forma, permitiendo que Tau viaje e infecte células sanas. En ratones sin Arc, la propagación de Tau casi desapareció.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra una nueva forma en que el Alzheimer se propaga en el cerebro. En lugar de eliminar toda la Tau, los futuros tratamientos podrían bloquear estas vesículas con Tau antes de que lleguen a células sanas. Esto podría ralentizar el daño causado por el Alzheimer. La investigación también mostró que Arc ayuda a que las células enfermas vivan más tiempo al liberar Tau, indicando que la proteína tiene efectos buenos y malos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren encontrar formas de evitar que las vesículas con Tau entren en neuronas sanas sin afectar las funciones buenas de Arc. También deben confirmar si este proceso ocurre igual en humanos. Si es así, nuevos tratamientos podrían frenar el avance de la enfermedad, especialmente en etapas tempranas.

**Frase clave** – Científicos descubrieron que la proteína cerebral Arc ayuda a la propagación de proteínas Tau en Alzheimer, abriendo un camino para frenar la enfermedad bloqueando esa propagación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of interconnected glowing neurons shaped like delicate branches, with small, translucent vesicles carrying bright, puzzle-piece shaped proteins traveling along the branches—some vesicles gently blocked by soft, protective light barriers symbolizing a hopeful barrier to disease spread, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Daily briefing: Ovaries start a second job after menopause
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02065-8The organs might begin a role in the immune system after egg production stops. Plus, precise gene-editing of human embryos and a game to terraform Mars.
articles.content:
The organs might begin a role in the immune system after egg production stops. Plus, precise gene-editing of human embryos and a game to terraform Mars.

Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

A human embryo ‘base edited’ so that it can’t produce a key protein (right), fails to form the mass of cells that gives rise to tissues and organs. A non-edited embryo (left) shows the cells (cyan). Credit: Katarina Harasimov, Oliver Bower and Kathy Niakan, Loke Centre for Trophoblast Research, Univ. Cambridge

For the second time this month, researchers have used base editing — a precise gene-editing technique — to alter the DNA of human embryos. The team found that a key protein called NANOG plays a part in embryo development that had not been seen in mouse studies. The finding highlights the need to study human embryos rather than relying on animal models, says developmental biologist Janet Rossant. But it has also renewed the urgency of ethical discussion over how base editing of embryos should be used.

Nature | 5 min read

Reference: Nature paper

The United States has historically led other nations in peppering the ocean with monitoring instruments and supporting cutting-edge oceanography research. Now cuts and threats of cuts have researchers worried it is no longer a reliable partner. The US National Science Foundation (NSF) has pulled back from a plan to dismantle an array of hundreds of marine instruments known as the Ocean Observatories Initiative. But another programme facing immediate crisis is a network of robotic floats dedicated to marine biogeochemistry, funded by the NSF and part of a global flotilla called Argo.

Nature | 5 min read

After the ovaries ramp down their reproductive role, releasing eggs and sex hormones, they might become more important to the immune system. Evidence from people and mice suggest that genes and proteins associated with immune activity are more active and prevalent in postreproductive ovaries — though it’s unclear whether it's a beneficial change. “We really owe it to women’s health to study this period of time,” says reproductive biologist and study co-author Francesca Duncan.

Science | 7 min read

Reference: Molecular Human Reproduction paper

Question of the week As AI reaches ever further into the practice of science, we’d love to know which aspects of your work you would hate to pass over to a chatbot or robot. Are there parts of your job you are excited to be doing yourself, that AI tools can’t help with, or that you don’t want to stop doing? How might AI affect your motivation and skills as a scientist? Please take our short survey and tell us what you think — some responses might be considered for publication in Nature. Take the survey

Features & opinion

Illustrations by Petra Péterffy

For humans to make the leap to Mars full time, scientists first have to ‘terraform’ the red planet, altering the conditions step-by-step to make its environment habitable. The possibility of doing so is gaining traction among some scientists. Now, you too can try your hand at terraforming with Nature’s game. At each stage of this experimental adventure, you can try out different strategies to give the icy planet a glow up. But choose wisely, not all options lead to humanity’s home away from home.

Nature | Interactive game

The Universe is reset one too many times in the latest short story for Nature’s Futures series.

Nature | 6 min read

Andrew Robinson’s pick of the top five science books to read this week includes the natural history of dawn and dusk and the ethical complexities of working with lab animals.

Nature | 4 min read

Cyberattacks on medical AI systems could allow criminals to steal sensitive patient information from the systems’ underlying data sets. For example, researchers showed that a model, which makes cancer-related predictions from routine blood-test data, could reveal the cancer-diagnosis status of an individual in the training set. And people who are in a minority in the data — for reasons as varied as race, sex, or even how their medical image was taken — are at most risk of these kinds of ‘membership inference attacks’.

Nature Podcast | 20 min listen

Subscribe to the Nature Podcast on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or YouTube Music, or use the RSS feed.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant scientific discovery about the ovaries potentially playing a new role in the immune system after menopause, which could have broad implications for women's health. It also discusses the ethical and scientific advances in precise gene-editing of human embryos, emphasizing the importance of human-specific research. These findings represent meaningful progress in health science with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Ovaries may help the immune system after menopause, new research shows
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ovaries are known for producing eggs and hormones during a woman’s reproductive years. After menopause, when egg production stops, scientists have wondered what happens to the ovaries.

**What happened** – Recent research found that after menopause, ovaries might take on a new role connected to the immune system. Studies in both people and mice showed that genes and proteins linked to immune activity become more active in ovaries that no longer produce eggs. This discovery came alongside other scientific advances, like new gene-editing techniques used to study human embryos more precisely.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it suggests ovaries don’t just stop working after menopause; they might help protect the body in a different way. Understanding this could lead to better health care for women after menopause, a time not well studied before. Also, the gene-editing work shows why studying human embryos directly is needed, as animal studies don’t always tell the full story.

**What’s next step** – Scientists want to learn more about how ovaries support the immune system and whether this change helps or harms health. The research may also guide new treatments to improve women's health after menopause. Meanwhile, the gene-editing discoveries call for careful discussions about how to use this technology responsibly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After menopause, ovaries may switch roles to boost the immune system, opening new paths for understanding and improving women’s health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les ovaires pourraient soutenir le système immunitaire après la ménopause, révèle une nouvelle étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ovaires produisent des ovules et des hormones pendant les années de reproduction d’une femme. Après la ménopause, lorsque la production d’ovules cesse, les scientifiques se demandent quel rôle jouent les ovaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une recherche récente a montré qu’après la ménopause, les ovaires pourraient jouer un nouveau rôle lié au système immunitaire. Des études sur des humains et des souris ont révélé que des gènes et des protéines liés à l’activité immunitaire deviennent plus actifs dans les ovaires qui ne produisent plus d’ovules. Cette découverte s’ajoute à d’autres avancées scientifiques, comme les techniques précises d’édition génétique utilisées pour étudier les embryons humains.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle suggère que les ovaires ne s’arrêtent pas de fonctionner après la ménopause, mais pourraient aider à protéger le corps autrement. Mieux comprendre cela pourrait améliorer les soins pour les femmes après la ménopause, une période peu étudiée auparavant. De plus, l’édition génétique montre pourquoi il est nécessaire d’étudier directement les embryons humains, car les études sur les animaux ne racontent pas toujours toute l’histoire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent en savoir plus sur la façon dont les ovaires soutiennent le système immunitaire et si ce changement est bénéfique ou non pour la santé. Cette recherche pourrait aussi guider de nouveaux traitements pour améliorer la santé des femmes après la ménopause. Par ailleurs, les découvertes sur l’édition génétique appellent à des discussions responsables sur l’usage de cette technologie.

**En une phrase** – Après la ménopause, les ovaires pourraient changer de rôle pour renforcer le système immunitaire, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre et améliorer la santé des femmes.
articles.personality_title_es: Los ovarios podrían apoyar al sistema inmunológico tras la menopausia, según nueva investigación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los ovarios producen óvulos y hormonas durante los años reproductivos de una mujer. Después de la menopausia, cuando la producción de óvulos se detiene, los científicos han querido saber qué sucede con los ovarios.

**Qué pasó** – Una investigación reciente descubrió que después de la menopausia, los ovarios podrían asumir un nuevo papel relacionado con el sistema inmunológico. Estudios en personas y ratones mostraron que genes y proteínas vinculados a la actividad inmune se vuelven más activos en ovarios que ya no producen óvulos. Este hallazgo se suma a otros avances científicos, como técnicas precisas de edición genética para estudiar embriones humanos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque sugiere que los ovarios no dejan de funcionar después de la menopausia, sino que podrían ayudar a proteger el cuerpo de otra manera. Comprender esto podría mejorar la atención médica para mujeres después de la menopausia, una etapa poco estudiada. Además, el trabajo en edición genética muestra por qué es necesario estudiar directamente los embriones humanos, ya que los estudios en animales no siempre cuentan toda la historia.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren aprender más sobre cómo los ovarios apoyan al sistema inmunológico y si este cambio es beneficioso o no para la salud. La investigación también podría guiar nuevos tratamientos para mejorar la salud de las mujeres tras la menopausia. Mientras tanto, los descubrimientos en edición genética requieren debates cuidadosos sobre el uso responsable de esta tecnología.

**Resumen en una frase** – Después de la menopausia, los ovarios podrían cambiar de función para fortalecer el sistema inmunológico, abriendo nuevas vías para entender y mejorar la salud de las mujeres.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two stylized ovaries resting on a soft glowing background, subtly transforming into intricate, delicate immune cells and molecular patterns that gently intertwine, rendered in natural, muted tones of soft pinks, creams, and gentle greens, evoking a peaceful transition from fertility to immune function.

articles.title: Humans sleep the least of all apes – is it the secret to our success?
articles.summary: Sleep is essential, yet humans have evolved to need so little of it. When evolutionary anthropologist David Samson delved into our ancient past to find the reasons why, he discovered surprising ways to get a better night’s rest
articles.content:
Sleep is essential, yet humans have evolved to need so little of it. When evolutionary anthropologist David Samson delved into our ancient past to find the reasons why, he discovered surprising ways to get a better night’s rest

Humans have evolved to sleep less than other primates Michael Kirkham

I am napping in a chimpanzee nest, 12 metres above the ground in Uganda’s Toro-Semliki Wildlife Reserve. Sleep comes easier than you might expect. The nest is surprisingly well-engineered: springy, cupped and stable in a way that feels less like a pile of branches than a purpose-built piece of furniture.

Then I wake up. I am suddenly, acutely aware that I am not in bed, but balanced high in the canopy.

I had climbed into the nest to investigate one of life’s least appreciated evolutionary problems: sleep, a paradoxical state that is essential yet renders the sleeper extremely vulnerable. In particular, I wanted to find out why human sleep is so weird. We do it far less than a primate like us ought to, so how did we evolve to need so little? Our sleep patterns are strange, too, with an unusually high proportion of the rapid eye movement stage.

By delving into humanity’s deep evolutionary past, as well as studying other primates, I hoped to find out why humans not only began to sleep less, but also reconfigured the way we do it. In the process, I discovered the long-overlooked role of sleep in humanity’s success, and found surprising answers to the question of whether we should aim to “palaeo sleep”, the way ancient humans did .

Sleep is wonderful, but also a terrible idea. It is biologically indispensable, critical for cognition, immune function, memory, attention and health. But a sleeping animal isn’t able to defend itself or watch for danger. Sleep also steals time that could be devoted to productive activities, such as finding a mate or foraging.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

That tension runs through the animal kingdom, meaning that species don’t evolve to maximise sleep, but instead end up with a compromise. Across mammals, sleep duration varies drastically between species, from 2 hours a day for African elephants to 20 hours for the pocket mouse. That disparity is the result of a messy tradeoff determined by predation risk, metabolic needs, foraging time and the safety of the sleeping site. Predators, for example, sleep longer than prey animals.

Imagine asking a biologist, with no knowledge of human sleep habits, to predict how much a primate like us should be getting. Give them our body size, brain size, reproductive schedule, place on the primate family tree and typical slumber environment, and they would predict around 9.5 hours. This is about 35 per cent more than the 7 hours that, after we control for type of society (small off-grid vs large on-grid), seems to be close to the human average. In fact, humans sleep less than any of the 30 primate species for which comparable data has been assembled.

This isn’t a small deviation that can be explained away by invoking cultural traditions or modern overstimulation. As I outline in my new book, The Sleepless Ape, it is a deep evolutionary outlier, and any serious explanation of human evolution has to reckon with that fact.

Human sleep is very unusual and short, compared to that of other great apes David Turnley/Corbis/VCG via Getty Images

The strangeness doesn’t stop with how little we sleep. It extends to how our sleep is built.

In humans and many other mammals, sleep cycles through two main phases: rapid eye movement sleep (REM) and non-rapid eye movement sleep (NREM). It is during the NREM phase that our deepest sleep occurs, associated with physical restoration, strengthening of the immune system and the consolidation of memories, especially declarative memories (those to do with recalling facts and events).

REM, by contrast, is associated with vivid dreaming, temporary muscle paralysis and wake-like brain activity that results in a paradoxical state in which the brain is highly metabolically active while many skeletal muscles are switched off. This phase appears to play an important role in emotional processing and integrating some memories and skills into brain pathways.

In REM sleep, thermoregulation – the process by which the body maintains its core temperature – is largely turned off because the brain temporarily prioritises dream-related neural activity and muscle paralysis over body-temperature control. So, in this state, body temperature is more dependent on the surrounding environment and, in some ways, the sleeper is maximally compromised.

Yet humans, despite sleeping less overall, devote a remarkably high proportion of their shorter nights to REM.

This finding came from a 2018 study I carried out with my colleague Charles Nunn, who studies evolutionary medicine at Duke University, North Carolina, which compared the sleep characteristics of 30 primates, including chimps, orangutans, macaques and lemurs. We found that humans have the highest percentage of REM sleep among the primates sampled. That doesn’t mean we spend dramatically more total time in REM than expected. In fact, human REM duration was only modestly above the predicted average.

The real shift lies in reduced NREM duration. Our model predicted 8.4 hours of NREM for humans, but the actual time typically spent in this phase is 5.4 hours. We are, in effect, a primate that has compressed the night largely by cutting back on NREM while preserving a relatively large share of REM.

That is a very odd situation. If evolutionary forces resulted in reduced sleep time for a particular species, you might expect the most costly components to be shaved down first. But humans seem to have done almost the reverse. We became short sleepers without surrendering the proportion of sleep most associated with dreaming and a brain that remains metabolically active through the night.

From one angle, this looks almost reckless. Why would natural selection produce an ape that sleeps less, while protecting such a costly state? From another angle, it looks like a clue. Perhaps what was under selection wasn’t less sleep for its own sake, but a new kind of sleep economy: shorter, more efficient and carefully reweighted towards the cognitive functions that mattered most in our lineage.

That possibility becomes more plausible when we consider the places where humans sleep compared with other primates. Where you sleep matters: whether you can conceal yourself, whether you can cling safely, whether your group offers protection and how much danger will result from being unconscious in your environment.

Great apes solved some of these problems by building nests. Those sleeping platforms probably improved sleep quality by increasing physical comfort and reducing the danger posed by predators. Some favoured trees often selected by chimpanzees, like Cynometra or African ironwood, also have mosquito-repellent properties. Barbara Fruth and Gottfried Hohmann of the Max Planck Institute for Animal Behaviour in Radolfzell, Germany, have even made the case that nest-building resulted in a great cognitive leap forward for great apes by enabling a better night’s rest, which could have underpinned the development of object-manipulation skills.

Chimpanzees and other great apes gain huge benefits from sleeping in nests they build in trees Cyril Ruoso/Minden Pictures/Alamy

Perched in that chimpanzee nest above Toro-Semliki, I begin to sense the scale of the transition our lineage eventually made. At some point in our deep past, our ancestors left this world of arboreal mattresses and moved towards something more precarious: sleeping habitually on the ground. In doing so, we became the most sleepless of them all.

Humans are the only primates that regularly snooze on terra firma across age and sex classes (although some male gorillas and some chimpanzees do also opt for the ground, where the risk of attack by predators is low). This move from the trees seems to have become a defining feature by the time of our ancestor Homo erectus around 1.8 million years ago.

From the perspective of a chimpanzee nest, the ground looks like a terrible idea. On the savannah or woodland floor, sleepers become more accessible to predators and more exposed to hostile rivals.

Human sleep paradox

This is why the human sleep paradox – the fact that our lineage seems to have been under selection to get it done faster, more efficiently and under riskier conditions than our closest relatives – is so interesting. It prevents us from telling a lazy story: that humans are tired because modern life is busy, or that our sleep troubles began with electricity, or that the 7-hour average is merely a cultural aberration.

The comparative record suggests something much deeper: that we are a lineage that departed sharply from the primate norm long before alarm clocks, shift work or glowing screens. The question isn’t whether modernity has damaged sleep, but what kind of evolutionary pathway could have produced such an unusual sleeper in the first place.

The answer, I think, begins where the nest in the trees ends.

Chimpanzees solved some of the problems of sleep by perfecting life in the trees. Humans solved it by abandoning trees and then remaking their nights on the ground. In doing so, they entered a far riskier situation that, over time, they transformed through cooperation, shelter, fire and shared vigilance into something no other ape had ever built: a social sleep niche. Only then does the oddity of human sleep begin to make sense.

At some point in the evolution of early Homo, the place where bodies slept became a collective technology: a defended patch of ground, a firelit perimeter, a shared shelter, a zone of lowered risk created by many individuals at once. That shift matters because sleep is never just a trait of a brain in isolation. It also depends on the environment. And in early humans, that environment increasingly became something they built together.

One way to think about this is through what I call the sleep exophenotype. Biologists sometimes refer to the extended phenotype, the idea that the effects of an organism’s genes don’t stop at the skin. A beaver’s dam is the classic example: although the dam isn’t part of the beaver’s body, it is still an evolved expression of beaver behaviour that reshapes the animal’s world in ways that feed back into survival and reproduction.

Human sleep sites belong in that same family of ideas, but with a specifically social twist. A sleep exophenotype is the external sleep environment that organisms create, modify or inherit in ways that alter the quality, safety, timing or expression of sleep. In humans, this exophenotype was communal. It was built out of bodies, technologies, habits and mutual dependence.

To describe this, I use the acronym SHELL, which is the suite of protective human sleep innovations: shelter, hearth, environmental preparation, light and lookouts. Humans wrapped sleep inside a socially engineered shell. This idea grew, in part, out of my work with the Hadza, a hunter-gatherer society in Tanzania whose way of life can offer clues about the kinds of sleep environments humans may have experienced before agriculture and industrialisation (see “Should we “palaeo sleep”?“) .

Hadza hunter-gatherers from Tanzania can give clues about how people may have slept before the advent of industrialisation and farming David Samson

My research assistant Ibrahim Mabulla and I lived and collaborated with the Hadza for a summer and used wearable devices to measure their sleep. We learned that group sleeping reduced the odds that every individual would be equally vulnerable at once, with people waking at different times across the night, helping to distribute vigilance over that time. In fact, there were only 18 minutes across 20 nights in which all the group members were asleep simultaneously. At least one person was awake at almost any given time throughout the night.

Human sleep, in other words, appears to have become distributed across the group. Shelters and hearths provided warmth, deterrence and a social centre of gravity. Environmental preparation – clearing ground, organising bodies, reusing favoured camp locations and collectively shaping the wider sleep site – made nighttime spaces more predictable and defensible. Light from fire altered both visibility and evening sociality, extending the human niche into the dark, and grass huts buffered nighttime thermal variation. In short, we changed the ecology around sleep.

Once sleep became embedded in that shell, social sleep could do something extraordinary: it could make terrestrial sleep workable, accommodating ever-increasing group sizes over evolutionary time.

This may be one of the most underappreciated transitions in human evolution. We often celebrate bipedalism, fire and language as the great enabling revolutions of our lineage. But social sleep belongs on that list. Yes, some other social mammals, such as baboons, also benefit from group sleeping and collective vigilance, but the human case is unusually elaborate.

A protected sleep shell would have allowed early humans to gain the cognitive benefits of shorter, more efficient, REM-rich sleep, such as improved creativity and innovation, without paying the full costs of vulnerability.

Huts built by the Hadza people make sleeping safer and more comfortable than spending the night out in the open David Samson

Once early humans could reliably manufacture warmer, quieter and safer nocturnal microhabitats, they were no longer bound to locations close to the equator in quite the same way. All this would have been a crucial pre-adaptation to prepare humans for migrating to latitudes away from equatorial Africa and eventually thriving in almost every ecology on Earth. Before humans colonised new continents, we first had to learn how to carry a habitable night with us.

Seen this way, the sleep site isn’t a footnote to human evolution. It is one of its hidden engines, creating a nighttime world within which humans could survive and then prosper. Out of that shell came not just safer sleep, but also a platform for cognition, innovation and expansion. We became modern humans not only by changing our bodies and brains, but by constructing external niches that let those bodies and brains do something unprecedented: sleep briefly, sleep deeply, sleep socially and wake ready to build the incredible world we now inhabit.

Should we “palaeo sleep”? It is tempting to imagine that the answer to sleep problems in today’s busy, large-scale societies is to “go palaeo” and adopt the habits of our hunter-gatherer ancestors: sleep communally outside and forget mattresses, blackout curtains and sleep apps. But the lesson from the Hadza, one of the world’s best-studied hunter-gatherer societies, is more subtle than that – as I discovered when I lived among these people to study their sleep. The Hadza are, of course, modern humans, not living fossils. But as East African hunter-gatherers living year-round in environments broadly similar to those faced by people in that region tens of thousands of years ago, they offer a useful comparative model for thinking about some of the ecological challenges early humans had to solve. Hadza adults in Tanzania typically report a satisfying night’s rest. When questioned, only a small fraction responded that they didn’t like their sleep. Yet objectively, their nights don’t look like the idealised Western fantasy of perfect rest. Like other foragers, they sleep relatively short hours by the standards of post-industrial societies, and their nights are often fragmented by awakenings, movement and noise. In other words, ancestral-style sleep isn’t a recipe for long, uninterrupted, blissfully sealed-off slumber. So why does it seem to work so well? Part of the answer may be that the Hadza aren’t sleeping more, but sleeping more in sync with their environment. Their sleep patterns are attuned to daily cycles of lightness and darkness compared with those typically seen in people living in industrialised societies, thanks to brighter days, darker nights and more regular daily rhythms. This shows up as a higher circadian function index, a measure of how well a person’s daily circadian rhythms align with the prevailing light conditions. That stronger alignment may help their sleep feel better, even if it is shorter and more broken up. It may also help explain the paradox of Hadza sleep. You can tolerate a shorter and more interrupted night if sleep lands at the right time in terms of your body’s rhythms. So the Hadza don’t teach us that prehistoric sleep was longer, softer or more luxurious. They teach us something more important. The goal shouldn’t be to recreate hunter-gatherer sleep, but to recover some of the circadian synchrony that our busy lives have made so easy to lose – not by obsessing over lost sleep, but doubling down on getting in sync.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a well-researched evolutionary perspective on human sleep, revealing how humans evolved to sleep less but more efficiently, with significant cognitive and social benefits. It highlights the broad significance of sleep patterns for human health, cognition, and social cooperation, providing substantial context and new understanding that could inspire better sleep practices and appreciation of human evolution.
articles.personality_title: How humans evolved to sleep less but smarter than other apes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Sleep is essential for health and brain function, yet humans sleep less than any other primate. Scientists have long wondered why this is the case and how our sleep patterns evolved differently from those of our closest animal relatives.

**What happened** – Evolutionary anthropologist David Samson studied human ancestors and compared their sleep with that of other primates like chimpanzees. He found that humans not only sleep fewer hours but also spend a larger part of their sleep in the dreaming phase called REM sleep. Unlike apes who build nests in trees, early humans began sleeping on the ground, which was riskier but led to new social behaviors such as group sleeping, building shelters, and using fire to protect and share their sleep environment.

**Impact** – This shift to sleeping on the ground and creating a safe, shared sleeping space helped humans sleep more efficiently despite sleeping less. Group sleeping divided night-time watchfulness among members, reducing vulnerability. This social way of sleeping likely supported the development of human creativity, memory, and cooperation, helping our ancestors thrive and spread to new environments. It also explains why human sleep is unique and more focused on brain functions linked to dreaming.

**What's next step** – Understanding our evolutionary sleep patterns encourages us to rethink modern sleep habits. Instead of trying to copy ancient sleeping styles exactly, we might focus on syncing our sleep better with natural light cycles and creating safer, more comfortable sleep environments. Further research with traditional societies like the Hadza can offer clues on balancing sleep quality with modern life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Humans evolved to sleep less but more efficiently by creating social and protected sleep environments that supported brain function and survival in risky settings.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment les humains ont évolué pour dormir moins mais mieux que les autres singes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le sommeil est essentiel pour la santé et le fonctionnement du cerveau, pourtant les humains dorment moins que tous les autres primates. Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps pourquoi et comment nos habitudes de sommeil ont évolué différemment de celles de nos plus proches parents animaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’anthropologue évolutionniste David Samson a étudié les ancêtres humains et comparé leur sommeil à celui d’autres primates comme les chimpanzés. Il a découvert que les humains dorment non seulement moins longtemps, mais passent aussi une plus grande partie de leur sommeil dans la phase de rêve appelée sommeil paradoxal (REM). Contrairement aux singes qui construisent des nids dans les arbres, les premiers humains ont commencé à dormir au sol, ce qui était plus risqué mais a entraîné de nouveaux comportements sociaux comme le sommeil en groupe, la construction d’abris et l’usage du feu pour protéger et partager leur environnement de sommeil.

**Impact** – Ce passage à un sommeil au sol et la création d’un espace de sommeil sûr et partagé ont permis aux humains de dormir plus efficacement malgré des heures de sommeil réduites. Dormir en groupe répartissait la vigilance nocturne entre les membres, réduisant la vulnérabilité. Cette manière sociale de dormir a probablement soutenu le développement de la créativité, de la mémoire et de la coopération humaines, aidant nos ancêtres à prospérer et à se répandre dans de nouveaux environnements. Cela explique aussi pourquoi le sommeil humain est unique et plus centré sur les fonctions cérébrales liées aux rêves.

**Prochaine étape** – Comprendre nos rythmes de sommeil évolutifs nous invite à repenser nos habitudes modernes. Au lieu d’essayer de copier exactement les styles de sommeil anciens, nous pourrions chercher à mieux synchroniser notre sommeil avec les cycles naturels de lumière et à créer des environnements de sommeil plus sûrs et confortables. D’autres recherches auprès de sociétés traditionnelles comme les Hadza peuvent offrir des indices pour équilibrer qualité de sommeil et vie moderne.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les humains ont évolué pour dormir moins mais plus efficacement en créant des environnements sociaux et protégés qui soutiennent les fonctions cérébrales et la survie dans des conditions risquées.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo los humanos evolucionaron para dormir menos pero de forma más inteligente que otros simios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El sueño es esencial para la salud y el funcionamiento del cerebro, pero los humanos duermen menos que cualquier otro primate. Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo por qué y cómo nuestras costumbres de sueño evolucionaron de manera diferente a las de nuestros parientes animales más cercanos.

**Qué pasó** – El antropólogo evolutivo David Samson estudió a los antepasados humanos y comparó su sueño con el de otros primates como los chimpancés. Descubrió que los humanos no solo duermen menos horas, sino que también pasan una mayor parte de su sueño en la fase de sueños llamada sueño REM. A diferencia de los simios que construyen nidos en los árboles, los primeros humanos comenzaron a dormir en el suelo, lo que era más arriesgado pero llevó a nuevos comportamientos sociales como dormir en grupo, construir refugios y usar el fuego para proteger y compartir su entorno para dormir.

**Impacto** – Este cambio a dormir en el suelo y crear un espacio para dormir seguro y compartido ayudó a los humanos a dormir de manera más eficiente a pesar de dormir menos horas. Dormir en grupo dividía la vigilancia nocturna entre los miembros, reduciendo la vulnerabilidad. Esta forma social de dormir probablemente apoyó el desarrollo de la creatividad, la memoria y la cooperación humanas, ayudando a nuestros antepasados a prosperar y expandirse a nuevos ambientes. También explica por qué el sueño humano es único y más centrado en funciones cerebrales relacionadas con los sueños.

**Próximo paso** – Entender nuestros patrones evolutivos de sueño nos anima a repensar los hábitos modernos. En lugar de intentar copiar exactamente los estilos de sueño antiguos, podríamos enfocarnos en sincronizar mejor nuestro sueño con los ciclos naturales de luz y crear ambientes para dormir más seguros y cómodos. Más investigaciones con sociedades tradicionales como los Hadza pueden ofrecer pistas para equilibrar la calidad del sueño con la vida moderna.

**Mensaje clave en una frase** – Los humanos evolucionaron para dormir menos pero de manera más eficiente creando ambientes sociales y protegidos que apoyan la función cerebral y la supervivencia en condiciones riesgosas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, intricately woven chimpanzee nest high in a lush Ugandan forest canopy beside a softly glowing campfire surrounded by simple, circular huts on the forest floor, with subtle silhouettes of resting humans and watchful eyes in the dark, capturing the transition from arboreal nest-building to communal, firelit ground sleeping that symbolizes human evolutionary innovation in sleep.

articles.title: The skin compound that makes some more delicious to mosquitoes than others
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Mosquitoes don’t pick targets randomly. They run a multi-stage sensory scan, and new research is beginning to explain what that scan detects and why it favors some people over others. “It’s not a misconception: mosquitoes are attracted to some people more than others,” said Frederic Simard of France’s Institute […]
The post The skin compound that makes some more delicious to mosquitoes than others first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Mosquitoes don’t pick targets randomly. They run a multi-stage sensory scan, and new research is beginning to explain what that scan detects and why it favors some people over others.

“It’s not a misconception: mosquitoes are attracted to some people more than others,” said Frederic Simard of France’s Institute of Research for Development. “But we are not all magnets all the time.”

How mosquitoes home in on a target

The process works in stages. Carbon dioxide is the opening signal: female mosquitoes, which are the only ones that bite, detect exhaled CO2 from dozens of meters away, and it triggers their approach. Within about 10 meters (roughly 33 feet), odor takes over, blending with the CO2 signal to sharpen the attraction. Closer still, body heat and humidity come into the picture once the mosquito is within striking distance.

Swedish researcher Rickard Ignell, senior author of a recent study on the subject, describes CO2 as the first trigger: “We have known for over 100 years that mosquitoes are attracted by the carbon dioxide that we exhale. This is the first signal that triggers their behavior.” Within a few meters, he said, “mosquitoes will start detecting our odor, and in combination with carbon dioxide, this attracts them even more.”

The 27 compounds mosquitoes use to find you

Humans emit between 300 and 1,000 different odorous compounds. Ignell’s team set out to narrow that down. Releasing Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, the species responsible for spreading dengue and yellow fever, among 42 women in a lab, they identified 27 compounds the insects key in on.

One compound did more work than the others: 1-octen-3-ol, sometimes called mushroom alcohol, which forms during the breakdown of sebum, the oil our skin produces. The women the mosquitoes most preferred, including pregnant women in their second trimester, produced notably more of it. The degree of sensitivity surprised the researchers. “That even a small increase of this compound made a difference came as a surprise,” Ignell said.

What doesn’t determine who gets bitten

Not everything people believe about mosquito preference holds up. Blood type “has no scientific basis” as an attractant, Simard said; the few studies suggesting otherwise involved very small samples. Skin color, eye color, and hair color are also irrelevant.

What does matter beyond the odor compounds is the microbial ecosystem on the skin. “A soup of molecules produced by our microbiota is more, or less, appealing to mosquitoes,” Simard explained. The microbial communities living on skin shape each person’s odor profile, which is why two people in the same environment can have completely different experiences with mosquitoes.

Why beer makes you more attractive to mosquitoes

Drinking beer genuinely increases mosquito attraction, and the evidence is solid. Beer raises body temperature, increases exhaled CO2, and changes skin odor, three of the signals mosquitoes track. In a study in Burkina Faso where volunteers drank beer on one occasion and water on another, the Anopheles mosquito, which spreads malaria, showed a clear preference for the beer drinkers. A 2023 study in the Netherlands, with 465 volunteers, found that people who had drunk beer in the previous 24 hours were 1.35 times more attractive to female Anopheles mosquitoes.

Why this research has become more urgent

The stakes have grown as climate change pushes disease-carrying mosquito species into new geographic ranges. The tiger mosquito, a vector for the chikungunya virus, has expanded significantly northward in Europe; last year, chikungunya reached France’s Alsace region for the first time. “This risk is affecting more and more people,” Simard said.

Knowing which compounds are doing the work points toward better repellent formulations and clearer ways to identify who’s most at risk. The near-term advice is what it has always been: loose-fitting clothing, mosquito nets, repellent, lighter meals, and less alcohol when mosquitoes are active. The science behind those recommendations is now considerably more precise.

Source study: Scientific Reports— Age-dependent perception of floral emissions and the role of CO 2 in regulating nectar-seeking in mosquitoes

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on new scientific research identifying specific skin compounds that attract mosquitoes, which has broad public health significance by improving understanding of mosquito behavior and aiding development of better repellents. This can help reduce mosquito-borne diseases, a major global health concern, especially as climate change expands mosquito habitats.
articles.personality_title: Scientists identify skin compound that attracts mosquitoes to some people more than others
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mosquitoes don’t bite people randomly. They use a series of signals like carbon dioxide, body odor, heat, and humidity to find their targets. Scientists have been trying to understand why mosquitoes prefer some people over others.

**What happened** – Researchers in France and Sweden studied how mosquitoes detect humans. They tested 42 women and found mosquitoes focus on 27 skin compounds. One called 1-octen-3-ol, or mushroom alcohol, stood out. People with more of this compound, including pregnant women, attracted more mosquitoes. The study also confirmed that blood type, skin color, and hair color don’t affect mosquito attraction. Drinking beer was found to increase mosquito attraction because it raises body temperature, carbon dioxide output, and changes skin odor.

**Impact** – This research explains exactly what attracts mosquitoes, which is important because mosquitoes spread serious diseases like dengue, yellow fever, and malaria. As climate change pushes mosquitoes into new areas, knowing the key compounds helps create better repellents and protective methods. It also helps identify who is more at risk of bites and disease.

**What’s next step** – Scientists can now work on developing mosquito repellents that target these specific skin compounds. Public health advice may become more precise, focusing on reducing exposure to these attractants. Monitoring mosquito spread will continue to protect people as new regions face mosquito-borne illnesses.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have discovered a specific skin compound that makes some people more attractive to mosquitoes, opening the door to better ways to prevent bites and disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques identifient un composé de la peau qui attire davantage les moustiques chez certaines personnes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les moustiques ne piquent pas les gens au hasard. Ils utilisent une série de signaux comme le dioxyde de carbone, l’odeur corporelle, la chaleur et l’humidité pour trouver leurs cibles. Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre pourquoi certains attirent plus les moustiques que d’autres.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs en France et en Suède ont étudié comment les moustiques détectent les humains. Ils ont testé 42 femmes et identifié 27 composés de la peau ciblés par les moustiques. Un composé appelé 1-octen-3-ol, ou alcool de champignon, s’est distingué. Les personnes avec plus de ce composé, y compris les femmes enceintes, attiraient davantage les moustiques. L’étude a aussi confirmé que le groupe sanguin, la couleur de peau ou des cheveux n’ont pas d’effet. Boire de la bière augmente l’attraction des moustiques en élevant la température corporelle, la production de dioxyde de carbone et en modifiant l’odeur de la peau.

**Impact** – Cette recherche explique précisément ce qui attire les moustiques, ce qui est important car ils transmettent des maladies graves comme la dengue, la fièvre jaune et le paludisme. Avec le changement climatique, les moustiques s’étendent dans de nouvelles régions. Connaître ces composés clés aide à créer de meilleurs répulsifs et méthodes de protection, et à identifier qui est plus exposé aux piqûres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques peuvent maintenant développer des répulsifs ciblant ces composés spécifiques. Les conseils de santé publique pourront devenir plus précis, en réduisant l’exposition à ces attractifs. La surveillance de la propagation des moustiques continuera pour protéger les populations.

**Phrase clé** – Les chercheurs ont découvert un composé cutané qui rend certaines personnes plus attirantes pour les moustiques, ouvrant la voie à de meilleures façons d’éviter les piqûres et les maladies.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos identifican compuesto de la piel que atrae más a los mosquitos en algunas personas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los mosquitos no pican a las personas al azar. Usan señales como dióxido de carbono, olor corporal, calor y humedad para encontrar a sus víctimas. Los científicos han tratado de entender por qué algunos atraen más a los mosquitos que otros.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Francia y Suecia estudiaron cómo los mosquitos detectan a los humanos. Probaron con 42 mujeres y encontraron 27 compuestos en la piel que atraen a los mosquitos. Uno llamado 1-octen-3-ol, o alcohol de champiñón, fue el más importante. Las personas con más de este compuesto, incluidas mujeres embarazadas, atraían más mosquitos. El estudio también confirmó que el tipo de sangre, color de piel o cabello no afectan la atracción. Beber cerveza aumenta la atracción porque eleva la temperatura corporal, la cantidad de dióxido de carbono exhalado y cambia el olor de la piel.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación explica con precisión qué atrae a los mosquitos, lo que es importante porque transmiten enfermedades graves como dengue, fiebre amarilla y malaria. Con el cambio climático, los mosquitos llegan a nuevas áreas. Conocer estos compuestos clave ayuda a crear mejores repelentes y métodos de protección, y a saber quién corre más riesgo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos ahora pueden desarrollar repelentes que se enfoquen en estos compuestos específicos. Las recomendaciones de salud pública podrán ser más claras para reducir la exposición. Se seguirá monitoreando la expansión de los mosquitos para proteger a las personas.

**Frase clave** – Los investigadores descubrieron un compuesto en la piel que hace que algunas personas sean más atractivas para los mosquitos, lo que permitirá mejores formas de prevenir picaduras y enfermedades.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting showing a gentle, glowing human silhouette surrounded by subtle swirling wisps of carbon dioxide and delicate, glowing molecular shapes representing skin compounds like 1-octen-3-ol, with a few stylized female mosquitoes drawn as elegant, soft-edged outlines gently approaching the figure, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft pastels.
